title: Tocoyena bullata
intro: Tocoyena bullata, also known as the Brazilian gold-dust tree, is a species of flowering plant in the family Rubiaceae. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues, due to its reported anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Tocoyena bullata include flavonoids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, as well as alkaloids and phenolic acids, which are thought to contribute to its medicinal effects. In traditional medicine, Tocoyena bullata is often used to prepare teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat a range of health issues, including respiratory problems and skin conditions.
taxonomy: Tocoyena bullata is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Rubiaceae. It is a member of the Rubiaceae family, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the madder family or coffee family. Within the Rubiaceae family, Tocoyena bullata is part of the genus Tocoyena, which consists of a group of flowering plants native to Central and South America. The genus Tocoyena is further classified into the tribe Cinchoneae, which contains a number of plant species native to the Americas. Tocoyena bullata is specifically classified as Tocoyena bullata (Cogn.) Standl., which reflects its unique characteristics and classification within the plant kingdom.

constituents: Tocoyena bullata contains a variety of bioactive compounds including alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids. These compounds contribute to its traditional medicinal uses and have been studied for their potential health benefits. Flavonoids present in the plant, such as kaempferol and quercetin, have been found to possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Alkaloids liketoberine and tocoyanine have been isolated from Tocoyena bullata and have shown potential as antimicrobial agents.
